agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO's description is a high-level vision statement from an all-hands meeting — not a formal specification. It is intentionally vague on details, thresholds, and constraints.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing / Rate Management",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of delivery request volume relative to capacity — exact definition unspecified (geographic? temporal? per-route?)" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme or irrational pricing" },
      { "term": "Real-time", "definition": "Price adjustments occur continuously or near-continuously in response to demand signals, not in batch or scheduled intervals" },
      { "term": "Fully automated", "definition": "No human approval step in the pricing adjustment loop under normal operating conditions" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must adjust in response to changes in demand level",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core business policy of the product. A VP could decide to pause dynamic pricing or switch to fixed pricing. It is a policy choice, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a measurable change in demand, the system produces a different price than the baseline/previous price within a defined time window"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "When demand increases, price must increase relative to the baseline price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a pricing policy. Leadership could decide to absorb demand spikes without raising prices as a competitive strategy.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand metric D2 > D1, the computed price P2 >= P1 (monotonically non-decreasing with demand)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "When demand decreases, price must decrease relative to the baseline price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a pricing policy. Leadership could set a price floor or decide not to lower prices below cost.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand metric D2 < D1, the computed price P2 <= P1 (monotonically non-increasing as demand falls)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real time — not in delayed batches or manual review cycles",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational policy. The business could decide to switch to batch pricing or add manual approval. 'Real-time' is also undefined — could mean seconds or minutes.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a demand change event, a new price is computed and available to consumers within a defined SLA (e.g., seconds, not hours)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The pricing system must operate without manual human intervention in the normal adjustment loop",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation is a design choice. The business may later require human-in-the-loop for certain conditions (regulatory review, extreme events). A VP could mandate manual approval above certain thresholds.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price adjustments are applied without requiring human approval or manual trigger under standard operating conditions"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Guardrails must exist to prevent prices from reaching extreme values (too high or too low)",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Guardrail thresholds are configurable business policy. The specific cap/floor values will change over time. However, the EXISTENCE of some bound is strongly implied.",
      "testable_assertion": "The system enforces a maximum price ceiling and a minimum price floor; no computed price may exceed the ceiling or fall below the floor"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The computed price must never be zero or negative",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is economically nonsensical and would represent data corruption or a broken pricing algorithm. You cannot pay the customer to deliver their package. This is a physical/economic constraint, not a policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "For all inputs, the final computed delivery price P > 0"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The pricing strategy must balance competitiveness with profitability — prices should not be so high as to lose all customers or so low as to operate at a loss",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a strategic business objective, not a hard constraint. The acceptable margin range is a policy decision that leadership will tune.",
      "testable_assertion": "The computed price for any delivery remains within a configured competitive range (above cost floor, below market-exit ceiling)"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes to an unprecedented level (e.g., natural disaster, holiday surge)", "expected_behavior": "Guardrails cap the price at a maximum ceiling; price does not increase unboundedly" },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero in a region", "expected_behavior": "Price decreases to the floor but never reaches zero or negative; system remains in a valid state" },
    { "scenario": "Demand signal is unavailable or corrupted (sensor failure, data pipeline outage)", "expected_behavior": "UNSPECIFIED — CEO description does not address failover pricing. Critical gap." },
    { "scenario": "Rapid demand oscillation (demand flips high/low every few seconds)", "expected_behavior": "UNSPECIFIED — no mention of smoothing, dampening, or hysteresis to prevent price whiplash" },
    { "scenario": "Two simultaneous orders in the same region see different prices due to mid-calculation update", "expected_behavior": "UNSPECIFIED — no mention of price consistency guarantees within a time window or for concurrent requests" },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price gouging laws triggered during an emergency", "expected_behavior": "UNSPECIFIED — no mention of legal/regulatory compliance constraints on dynamic pricing" },
    { "scenario": "Customer sees a price, but by the time they confirm, the price has changed", "expected_behavior": "UNSPECIFIED — no mention of price lock/quote validity window" }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Dynamic pricing directly impacts revenue and margin. Correct implementation increases revenue during high demand and maintains volume during low demand. Incorrect guardrails could cause revenue loss (caps too low) or customer churn (caps too high).",
    "operational": "Fully automated system reduces manual pricing overhead but introduces operational risk — pricing failures propagate instantly without human review. Monitoring and alerting become critical.",
    "user": "Customers experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Price volatility without explanation leads to negative perception (the 'Uber surge pricing backlash' pattern).",
    "risk": "High risk due to vagueness of specification. No defined guardrail values, no failover behavior, no regulatory compliance mention, no price consistency guarantees. Shipping this spec as-is will produce a system that 'works' but may not be 'correct' in edge cases that matter most.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Directly pay variable prices; trust and satisfaction depend on fairness perception and transparency" },
      { "group": "Operations / Dispatchers", "impact": "Must handle customer complaints about price changes; need visibility into why a price was set" },
      { "group": "Finance", "impact": "Revenue forecasting becomes harder with dynamic pricing; need audit trail of pricing decisions" },
      { "group": "Legal / Compliance", "impact": "Price gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and contractual obligations may constrain dynamic pricing in certain jurisdictions" },
      { "group": "Sales / Account Management", "impact": "Enterprise customers with negotiated rates — does DynaPricing override contract pricing?" },
      { "group": "Engineering / SRE", "impact": "Owns a fully automated system with direct financial impact; outage = pricing failure = revenue impact" }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is a 2-minute vision pitch, not a specification. It contains no quantitative thresholds, no defined demand metrics, no guardrail values, no failover strategy, no regulatory consideration, and no consistency guarantees. The word 'probably' before guardrails introduces uncertainty about whether guardrails are even a firm requirement. Almost every extracted rule required inference beyond what was explicitly stated. This is a starting point for requirements gathering, not a buildable spec.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Price adjusts based on demand (core concept, explicitly stated)",
      "Direction of adjustment: high demand = higher price, low demand = lower price (explicitly stated)",
      "System is automated (explicitly stated)",
      "Price must be positive (economic invariant, domain knowledge)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Guardrail specifics — 'probably' makes even their existence uncertain",
      "Definition of 'demand' — geographic scope, time window, measurement method all undefined",
      "Definition of 'real-time' — seconds? minutes? per-request?",
      "Failover behavior when demand signals are unavailable",
      "Price consistency and quote validity windows",
      "Interaction with existing contract/negotiated pricing",
      "Regulatory and legal constraints",
      "Whether 'competitive' and 'profitable' have quantified targets"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "DynaPricing applies to delivery fees, not product prices",
      "There is a concept of a 'baseline' or 'standard' price that dynamic adjustments modify",
      "Guardrails means both a ceiling (max price) and a floor (min price)",
      "'Fully automated' means no human-in-the-loop for normal operations, not that humans can never intervene",
      "The system applies to all deliveries unless scoped otherwise (no mention of exclusions)"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The word 'probably' before guardrails makes it unclear whether price bounds are a firm requirement or a nice-to-have. This is the single most important safety mechanism in the system and its status is ambiguous.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal / Compliance", "Finance"],
      "options": ["Treat guardrails as mandatory (recommended)", "Treat guardrails as optional and defer to a later phase"],
      "recommendation": "Treat guardrails as mandatory. A dynamic pricing system without bounds is a liability. Clarify with CEO."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No definition of 'demand' — is it order volume per hour? Orders per courier? Per geographic zone? Per route? The entire pricing model depends on how demand is measured, and this is completely unspecified.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Finance", "Operations / Dispatchers"],
      "options": ["Define demand as orders-per-hour per geographic zone", "Define demand as utilization ratio (orders / available couriers)", "Defer to product team for formal definition"],
      "recommendation": "Demand should likely be a utilization ratio (orders / available capacity) per geographic zone per time window. Escalate to product for confirmation."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No failover strategy specified. If demand data becomes unavailable (pipeline outage, sensor failure), what price does the system use? Last known price? Baseline? This is a critical operational gap.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering / SRE", "Customers", "Finance"],
      "options": ["Fall back to baseline/standard pricing", "Use last known dynamic price with a staleness timeout", "Halt order acceptance until data is restored"],
      "recommendation": "Fall back to baseline pricing with an alert. Never halt order acceptance due to a pricing data outage."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of regulatory compliance. Many jurisdictions have price gouging laws that cap price increases during emergencies. A fully automated system could violate these laws without safeguards.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal / Compliance", "Customers", "Finance"],
      "options": ["Add jurisdiction-aware price caps", "Apply a universal conservative cap", "Defer to legal review before launch"],
      "recommendation": "Legal review is mandatory before launch. Add jurisdiction-aware emergency caps as a guardrail category."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No price consistency guarantee. If a customer sees a quoted price of $12 and confirms 30 seconds later, can the price have changed to $18? This has massive UX and trust implications.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal / Compliance", "Sales / Account Management"],
      "options": ["Lock quoted price for a time window (e.g., 5 minutes)", "Price is always live — customer pays whatever price is current at confirmation", "Show estimated range, lock on confirmation"],
      "recommendation": "Lock quoted price for a reasonable window (2-5 minutes). Changing price between quote and confirmation destroys customer trust."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Relationship with contract/enterprise pricing is undefined. If an enterprise customer has a negotiated $5/delivery rate, does DynaPricing override it during high demand?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Sales / Account Management", "Finance", "Customers"],
      "options": ["DynaPricing never overrides contract rates", "DynaPricing applies but contract rates set a ceiling", "Contracts are renegotiated to include dynamic pricing terms"],
      "recommendation": "Contract rates should be honored. DynaPricing applies only to non-contract (spot) pricing. Escalate to Sales for confirmation."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
